summary: Soaring Dark Star and blazing Morning Dew dominate a high‑energy set
blurb: The show delivers an electric Dark Star, a blistering Morning Dew and a fiery Eleven. Jerry’s lead work is on fire while Phil locks into a deep musical world during the improvisations.
review:
Recorded on April 13, 1969 at the University of Colorado’s Ballroom, this performance captures the Dead in peak ’69 form. The first set rockets off with an incendiary Turn On Your Lovelight that segues into a ripping Doin' That Rag and then a soulful Good Morning Little School Girl before launching into one of the night’s highlights – a soaring Dark Star where Jerry Garcia delivers blistering runs and Mickey Hart adds spontaneous claps that underline the band’s telepathic interplay. Phil Lesh drifts in what reviewers call a “musical world” beneath the improvisation, providing a rock‑steady foundation. The momentum builds through Saint Stephen into The Eleven, where the quartet stretches time with relentless intensity, and the set closes on a heartfelt Death Don't Have No Mercy.

The second set keeps the fire burning with Alligator leading straight into an expansive drums/jam segment that epitomizes the era’s free‑form vibe. Although the Miller transfer has a few tape cuts – Lovelight ends abruptly and part of the Caution jam is missing – the overall sound remains balanced and clear, making this recording the most complete and listenable version available.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: All available analysis is positive, using language like 'energetic', 'soaring Dark Star', 'blazing Morning Dew' and 'fiery Eleven'. Consistent praise of Jerry's lead work and the overall high‑energy flow justifies a solid positive adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Full‑show soundboard capture with a clean Miller transfer; the most complete and balanced source despite minor tape cuts.

key_highlights: Soaring Dark Star with Jerry’s blistering solo and Mickey’s spontaneous claps
Blazing Morning Dew that showcases Garcia’s fiery lead work
The Eleven stretches time with relentless intensity
Jam‑heavy second half retains the classic ’69 exploratory spirit despite a few tape gaps

band_performance.Jerry: Delivers blistering runs on Dew, a soaring solo on Turn On Your Lovelight and crushing lead throughout The Eleven.
band_performance.Phil: Enters a "musical world" during Dark Star, providing a solid bass foundation that anchors the improvisations.
band_performance.Mickey: Adds spontaneous clapping during Dark Star, highlighting dynamic interplay; drums‑jam segment feels quintessentially period‑appropriate.
